_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Build partially succeeded but failed on cache. Checking if the public folder exists. Grepping generated CSS for responsive classes.

Three responsive issues identified. Link rows overflow horizontally without wrapping. Typography scales invert at the large breakpoint, causing overflow on tablets. Header gaps between medium and large breakpoints also need adjustment.

The fixed header offset assumes a 64px bar, yet mobile search pushes the header taller. The scroll padding does not account for this extra height. The title may truncate when the mobile navigation opens.

Section padding may be too tight on small screens. The site title lacks truncation on narrow viewports. Card overflow is clipped instead of wrapping. Flexbox with wrap and smoother typography scaling are the planned fixes.

The theme toggle lacks an explicit touch target, unlike the menu button. For the header, showing the navigation at the medium breakpoint with smaller text or truncating the title would help on tablets. Removing `overflow-hidden` from cards is risky because it clips focus rings. A larger mobile scroll padding would be preferable to dynamic JavaScript padding.

The theme toggle already has a 2.5rem touch target via its `touch-target` class. For the link rows, flex-wrap would place separators at line breaks and look awkward. Hiding separators during wrap is complex, so vertical stacking on mobile may be the cleaner fix.

The link-row fix will use flex-wrap with gap adjustments. Hiding separators on mobile would sacrifice visual grouping, so pipes at wrap boundaries are acceptable.

A full vertical stack on mobile would be too long for rows with many links. The chosen approach is `flex flex-wrap` with `gap-x-1 gap-y-2` and `items-center`. Separator pipes at line breaks remain an acceptable trade-off under the minimal scope constraint.

Adding `overflow-x-hidden` to the body prevents stray horizontal scrolls. The header search container needs `min-w-0` to stop flex shrink, and the site title should truncate on small screens. The main content offset is broken on mobile: the header is roughly 7.5rem tall once the search bar is included, but `pt-20` only adds 5rem, so content slides underneath the search field. A larger mobile top padding or a CSS custom property is needed to fix this.

The mobile header totals roughly 7.25rem once the search bar is included, so `pt-20` is insufficient. The search field sits inside `.site-header`, which explains why the offset fails on mobile. The plan is to increase mobile top padding to `pt-28` or a custom CSS variable, and to move the search input back into the header row on all screen sizes.

The mobile navigation expands as an overlay within the fixed header, so it does not push content downward. Scroll padding must account for the full header height including the mobile search bar. The tablet breakpoint is fine because the search remains inline. The large breakpoint text size was designed for two-column desktop density; on single-column layouts it is too large and causes overflow.

The text scale will be simplified to a single base size. Mobile and tablet will use `text-base`, `lg:text-sm` for two-column density, and `xl:text-base` for large screens. This removes the inverted breakpoint scaling that caused overflow.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Implementing responsive fixes through targeted CSS updates.

Padding and scroll offsets are being adjusted for mobile and tablet. Link rows will wrap cleanly. Typography will scale down on small screens to prevent overflow. Horizontal overflow clipping and search field constraints are also being added.

On mobile, pipe separators will be hidden so wrapped link rows stay clean. Tabletop links will keep their separators.

The site title will truncate on tablet screens to prevent overflow.

The header title will use `min-w-0` and `truncate` to prevent overflow on narrow screens. The mobile header top row will allow the title to flex and fill available space. Scroll padding will be set to 7rem on mobile and reduced above the medium breakpoint.

Mobile section padding will increase to `px-3 py-2 sm:px-4`. Section titles will scale down to `text-xl` on small screens. The theme toggle and menu button have duplicate classes that need cleanup. Safe area insets will be added for notched phones.

Typography will scale down to `text-base` on mobile and grow to `text-lg` on desktop. The body will get `overflow-x-clip` to prevent horizontal scrolling. Long link titles may need `whitespace-normal` since `inline-flex` currently blocks natural text wrapping.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Identifying issues: mobile main padding doesn't account for the fixed search bar; link rows don't wrap and use oversized text; separators break awkwardly when wrapped. Applying targeted CSS and layout fixes.

_**Agent (default)**_

Mobile and tablet responsiveness is updated. Here’s what was wrong and what changed:

### Issues fixed

1. **Content hidden under the header on mobile** — The fixed header includes the search bar below the top bar on screens under 768px, but main content only had `pt-20` (64px). Updated to `pt-28 md:pt-20` so content clears the full header.

2. **Horizontal overflow on link rows** — Rows with many links (e.g. Google services, design resources) didn’t wrap and caused sideways scrolling. Link rows now use `flex-wrap` with spacing, and long link text can break with `break-words`.

3. **Oversized mobile typography** — Links used `text-lg` (18px) on mobile/tablet, making rows harder to fit. Switched to `text-base` on small screens, keeping the denser `lg:text-sm` / `xl:text-lg` scale on desktop.

4. **Awkward pipe separators when wrapping** — `|` separators are hidden below `lg` and replaced with flex gaps; pipes remain on desktop where the two-column layout has room.

5. **Header crowding on small screens** — Site title truncates instead of pushing controls off-screen; search field gets `min-w-0` so it shrinks correctly on tablet.

6. **Section anchor scroll offset** — `scroll-padding-top` is `7rem` on mobile (taller header) and `5rem` from tablet up.

7. **Horizontal page scroll** — Added `overflow-x: clip` on `body` as a safety net.

Run `npm run dev` and resize the browser (or use device mode) to verify. If anything still feels off at a specific width, say which device or breakpoint and we can tune it.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The mobile search markup shows a label element with padding classes but no outer container wrapper.

The mobile navigation and top bar both wrap their content in `mx-auto max-w-content`. The mobile search label lacks this wrapper and only uses `px-4`.

The search likely stretches edge-to-edge across the full viewport width because it lacks the `max-w-content` container. Other sections remain centered within that width limit.

The fix is to wrap the mobile search label in a container with `mx-auto max-w-content` so it stays centered like the rest of the page.

Placing `max-w-content` directly on the label would shrink the top border to the content width. The better approach is a nested div with `border-t`, `mx-auto max-w-content px-4 py-2`, and a block-level label inside, matching the mobile navigation structure exactly.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Mobile search sits outside the `max-w-content` wrapper used elsewhere. Aligning it with the header and nav layout.

_**User**_

the piples are not correctly aligned in mobile and tablet

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Investigating the CSS and HTML structure for pipe separators to diagnose the misalignment on mobile and tablet.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Let me inspect how link rows and pipe separators are styled on smaller breakpoints.


_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/dd3/assets/css/main.css</summary>

</details>
</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2**</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/dd3/layouts/partials/link-item.html</summary>

</details>
</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2**</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/dd3/layouts/partials/block.html</summary>

</details>
</tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2**</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Several factors likely cause the misalignment on mobile and tablet.

Pipes are separate flex items. They do not receive the `min-h-11 py-1` padding that links and labels get under the coarse-pointer media query. This causes vertical misalignment. Flex wrapping can also orphan a pipe at the start of a new line.

The original design used inline flow. Switching to flex-wrap broke the natural text flow between pipes and links. Reverting to inline display on mobile and tablet would restore proper alignment.

Grouping links into inline segments would keep pipes attached to their preceding link, but that requires a template change. A simpler alternative is to set the row to inline flow with wrapping. Inline elements wrap at word boundaries, so pipes remain between links while the entire row wraps naturally.

Pure inline flow risks overflow for long links. `break-words` may not work on inline anchors without `overflow-wrap: anywhere`. The misalignment likely involves vertical centering, horizontal spacing, and pipes landing at line starts after wrapping.

A flex-only fix would still require grouping links and separators together in the HTML to prevent orphaned pipes after wrapping. The cleaner solution is to abandon flex entirely and use inline display for the entire row. Links and labels would become inline elements with `break-words`, while separators simply carry margin and faint styling. This restores natural text flow and avoids both vertical misalignment and wrapping artifacts.

In inline flow, wrapping occurs between whole links rather than at individual pipes. The separator stays attached to its preceding link, preventing orphaned pipes at line starts.

The original `inline-flex` links should still wrap normally, since flex containers participate in inline layout. The real problem was horizontal overflow from too many links on one line. Inline elements wrap as whole units, so the row should break cleanly between links without flex.

The original inline-flex links should have wrapped naturally at the container edge. The earlier assumption that inline layout would overflow is likely incorrect. The actual cause of the misalignment probably lies elsewhere.

The fix is to remove flex from `.link-row` and return to a block container with inline children. Links and labels should be plain `inline` elements rather than `inline-flex`, paired with proper line-height.

Separators should be inline with `leading-snug` and `align-middle` to stay on the text baseline. For coarse-pointer devices, touch padding should move to the row itself rather than individual links, since `min-h-11` on links distorts pipe alignment. A full-row `py-3` padding would preserve the correct baseline while keeping links touch-friendly.

The concrete CSS now sets `.link-row` to `min-h-6 py-2 leading-snug`, removes `min-h-11` and `min-w-6` from links and labels, and applies `break-words` to inline anchors and labels. Separators become plain inline elements with `mx-1 text-faint select-none`.

The coarse-pointer fix uses `py-3` on the row and `py-1.5` on links and labels. This adds tap area without flex item mismatches. `min-h-6` and `min-w-6` should be removed from links to avoid box-sizing issues in inline flow. `min-w-6` may remain for minimum tap width.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Flex layout treats pipes as separate items from links, so they misalign with taller touch targets on mobile. Switching link rows back to inline flow so pipes sit naturally with the text.


_**Agent (default)**_

_**Agent (default)**_

The misalignment came from using flexbox on link rows — each link and each `|` became its own flex item, and on touch devices the links got a taller `min-height` while the pipes did not.

Link rows are back to **inline text flow**, so pipes sit on the same baseline as the links around them, the way `Link | Link | Link` reads naturally. Touch padding is applied with vertical padding on links instead of fixed min-heights, so tap targets stay usable without throwing off the separators.

Refresh on mobile/tablet and the pipes should line up with the link text. If a specific row still looks off, point me at it and we can tune further.

_**User**_

for words, they must not split across 2 lines

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Link titles and labels should remain whole words without splitting across lines.

`break-words` currently allows mid-word line breaks. Replacing it with `nowrap` would prevent splitting but risk horizontal overflow for long titles.

Removing `break-words` will let words wrap only at spaces. Long strings may still need `overflow-wrap: anywhere`.

Each link is an inline box, so the entire title moves to the next line as one unit. Long unbroken tokens without spaces will overflow rather than split.

Removing `break-words` keeps multi-word titles intact while allowing natural word boundary wrapping. Using `white-space: nowrap` instead would force each link onto a single line, which is a different requirement.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Removing word-breaking so link text only wraps at spaces, not mid-word.

_**Agent (default)**_

Removed `break-words` from links and labels — that was allowing mid-word breaks when space ran out. Text now wraps only at spaces, so a word like "Generator" stays on one line. Added `hyphens-none` so the browser won't auto-hyphenate either.

Multi-word link titles can still wrap between words (e.g. "Trianglify Background" on one line, "Generator" on the next). Only individual words are kept intact.

---

_**User**_

allow the row to go to the next line for mobile and tablet

---

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Link rows currently use inline flow. They should wrap to the next line on mobile and tablet. There may be issues preventing this.

`overflow-hidden` on the card shouldn't block wrapping. The real issue may be that each `<a>` element is an inline box, preventing the row from breaking between links.

`overflow-x: clip` on the body might hide horizontal overflow. Flex wrap was previously removed due to pipe misalignment. A better approach may be wrapping each link and its separator in a span.

Wrapping each link and its separator in a span lets the row use flex-wrap. Pipes stay aligned with their links, and words remain intact. This targets mobile and tablet specifically, likely leaving desktop as a single line.

Desktop already uses a two-column grid, so rows are naturally narrower there. The plan is to apply flex-wrap only below the large breakpoint. Two options are being weighed: a CSS-only media query, or adding HTML wrappers around each link and separator to create atomic units.

The implementation now uses `inline-flex` with `white-space: nowrap` on each link item. This keeps multi-word labels intact as a single unit while allowing the entire group to wrap to the next line.

`white-space: nowrap` ensures multi-word links stay intact as single units while wrapping to the next line. Long single-word links may overflow, which is acceptable since words must not split. Desktop will keep inline flow for a dense horizontal layout.

A conflict emerged between keeping words intact and allowing the row to wrap. If links are wrapped as atomic units, multi-word titles could still break at spaces. The previous instruction to prevent word splitting may need to override the current request for row wrapping.

The approach changed. `white-space: nowrap` is dropped from each link item. Flex items wrap at their boundaries by default. Words inside a link can now wrap at spaces. This prevents mid-word splits while allowing the row to break between links.</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_
